headline: One Typewriter, One Finger, Infinite Creativity
meta_description: London artist James Cook creates intricate masterpieces using only typewriter keys, typing over a million characters per artwork.
summary_short: A 29-year-old London artist creates stunning works of art using only a typewriter and his right index finger—including a viral Wimbledon piece viewed 8 million times.
body:
When James Cook was in high school, an art assignment changed his life. He needed to create something with technology, so he asked his father to help find a typewriter. An older couple overheard the conversation in a charity shop and donated their 1950 Oliver Courier model that afternoon.

Now, thirteen years later, the 29-year-old from Canning Town, East London, has turned that gift into a full-time artistic practice. Working exclusively with his right index finger, Cook types over a million letters and symbols onto a single page to complete each piece.

His subjects range from famous cityscapes—Rome, Paris, New York—to celebrity portraits. But it was his recent "Wimbledon 2026" artwork that catapulted him to wider recognition. Created on a 1932 Smith Premier typewriter and taking roughly 378 hours of work over six weeks, the piece has been viewed more than 8 million times on social media. Tennis legends like Billie Jean King have commented on his work.

What makes the Wimbledon piece special isn't just its technical achievement—it's Cook's playful hidden details. Colored typewriter ribbons allowed him to create green grass using specially-made bands. Throughout the composition, a woman climbs a ladder seeking a glimpse of the match, a ball boy carries a ripped bag, and a man dressed as a strawberry wanders the court. Names of players and tennis terminology scatter across the design.

Small A4 prints take around five days to complete. Larger pieces take weeks. Yet Cook remains amazed at his own success: "I still can't believe sometimes that this is my job."
